KeyRotationEnabled.^description: You have {$COUNT} CMK does not have automatic rotation enabled. Rotating encryption keys helps reduce the potential impact of a compromised key because data encrypted with a new key can't be accessed with a previous key that might have been exposed.

AdminIsGrantor.^description: It is recommended to separate the usage permissions from administration permissions associated withthe key. Identify any publicly accessible AWS Key Management Service master keys and update their access policy in order to stop any unsigned requests made to these resources.

KeyInPendingDeletion.^description: [Warning] {$COUNT} KMS key is under Pending Deletion Stage. KMS keys cannot be recovered once deleted. Data encrypted under a KMS key is also permanently unrecoverable if the KMS key is deleted. If meaningful data has been encrypted under a KMS key scheduled for deletion, consider decrypting the data or re-encrypting the data under a new KMS key unless you are intentionally performing a cryptographic erasure.

DisabledKey.^description: [Informational], no action needed. {$COUNT} KMS key is under Disabled Stage.

GrantOverlyPermissive.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has grants with overly broad permissions. Grants should follow the principle of least privilege and only include the specific operations required by the grantee principal. Overly permissive grants increase security risk.
GrantOverlyPermissive.shortDesc: Limit grant permissions to minimum required

GrantWildcardPrincipal.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has grants with wildcard or overly broad grantee principals. Use specific IAM roles or users as grantee principals instead of account-level principals or wildcards to improve security and auditability.
GrantWildcardPrincipal.shortDesc: Use specific grantee principals in grants

GrantDuplicate.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has duplicate grants that should be cleaned up. Duplicate grants add unnecessary complexity and should be retired or revoked. Use the Name parameter when creating grants to avoid duplicates.

KeyPolicyCrossAccount.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has cross-account access in key policy. Review cross-account principals to ensure they are intentional and necessary. Unintended cross-account access can lead to security risks.

KeyPolicyWildcardPrincipal.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has wildcard principal in key policy, making the key publicly accessible. This is a critical security risk. Update the key policy to specify explicit principals instead of using wildcards.
KeyPolicyWildcardPrincipal.shortDesc: Remove wildcard principals from key policy

KeyPolicyWildcardAction.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has wildcard actions (kms:* or *) in key policy without appropriate conditions. Wildcard actions grant overly broad permissions. Use specific actions and add conditions to limit permissions.
KeyPolicyWildcardAction.shortDesc: Use specific actions instead of wildcards

KeyPolicyMissingRootAccess.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key policy does not include root account access. AWS requires root account access in key policies for key recovery and management. Add a statement granting the root account key management permissions.
KeyPolicyMissingRootAccess.shortDesc: Add root account access to key policy

GrantMissingEncryptionContext.^description: [Informational] {$COUNT} KMS key has grants without encryption context constraints. Consider using encryption context constraints to ensure the key is used only for its intended purpose. Use EncryptionContextEquals when possible.
GrantMissingEncryptionContext.shortDesc: Apply encryption context constraints to grants

GrantOldAge.^description: [Informational] {$COUNT} KMS key has grants older than 180 days. Review old grants to determine if they are still needed. Retire or revoke grants that are no longer required to reduce security risk.

KeyPolicySensitiveActionsNotRestricted.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key policy does not properly restrict sensitive actions (PutKeyPolicy, ScheduleKeyDeletion). These actions should be defined in the key policy with appropriate conditions, not delegated to IAM policies.

KeyPolicyNoConditions.^description: {$COUNT} KMS key has policy statements without conditions. Consider adding conditions to policy statements to limit permissions based on context (e.g., source IP, MFA, encryption context).
KeyPolicyNoConditions.shortDesc: Add conditions to key policy statements

KeyUnused.^description: [Informational] {$COUNT} KMS key appears to be unused based on heuristics (disabled state, no grants, minimal policy, old age). Review the key to determine if it can be deleted to reduce costs ($1/month per key).

KeyCentralizedManagement.^description: [Informational] {$COUNT} KMS key shows cross-account usage patterns indicating centralized key management. Review if this aligns with your organizational key management strategy (centralized vs decentralized).
